tweets.full_text:
A 19-year-old is making $164,000/month from creators who speak Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Korean, and English.

She only speaks one of them.

Pause at 0:22.

The screen recording opens on what looks like a college girl's bedroom. A psychology textbook face-down on the desk. A half-finished iced coffee. A Spotify playlist labeled "study" playing in the corner. The monitor on the desk has 5 tabs open across the top.

Each tab is an Instagram account.

Tab 1: a Spanish-language clean-girl beauty creator based in Mexico City. 412,000 followers. Her latest reel is a nighttime skincare routine. The caption is in Spanish.

Tab 2: an Italian-language minimalist fashion creator based in Milan. 287,000 followers. Latest reel is an outfit-of-the-day. The caption is in Italian.

Tab 3: a Portuguese-language fitness creator based in Rio. 519,000 followers. Latest reel is a beach workout. The caption is in Portuguese.

Tab 4: a Korean-language K-beauty creator based in Seoul. 334,000 followers. Latest reel is a 10-step skincare routine. The caption is in Hangul.

Tab 5: an English-language "soft girl" aesthetic creator based in Brooklyn. 198,000 followers. Latest reel is a morning routine. The caption is in English.

She's in her dorm in Boston. She's a sophomore. She speaks English and exactly 14 words of high school Spanish.

The hands on every reel are hers.

Same nail shape. Same ring on the right middle finger. Same gesture at the 11-second mark of each video — a small twist of the wrist when she introduces the product. Five different bedrooms. Five different lighting setups. Five different aesthetic systems. One pair of hands across all of them.

She doesn't write the captions. Claude does. Each account has a 3,200-word system prompt that locks the voice in its native language - the Mexican creator uses regional slang, the Milan creator uses the restrained vocabulary of Italian luxury fashion, the Rio creator uses Carioca Portuguese, the Seoul creator uses Korean honorifics correctly, the Brooklyn creator uses the chill American voice.

Claude writes 30-40 captions a week across all 5 accounts. ElevenLabs handles the voiceovers in each language - voice clones trained on 60-second audio samples she paid native speakers to record on Fiverr for $40 each.

She built the first persona 11 months ago. The Brooklyn one. Standard play - Higgsfield Soul ID, LoRA training, 90 source images, the usual stack. By month 3 the Brooklyn account was hitting 80K followers and her first brand deal closed at $1,800.

Then she did something almost nobody in this market does.

She didn't build a second persona in English. She built one in Spanish.

The insight was this: the AI UGC market in the US is saturated. Mid-tier brands get pitched by 60+ creators a week, and the conversion rate on cold outbound is dropping every quarter. Spanish-language brands in Mexico and Latin America have almost no AI creator presence - the market is wide open. Italian and Portuguese are even more underserved. Korean has the highest per-deal pricing in the world for skincare UGC because the K-beauty industry is massive and willing to pay.

She speaks none of these languages. She doesn't have to.

Claude handles the writing. ElevenLabs handles the voice. DeepL handles the contracts. A Cambly tutor she pays $24/hour handles cultural review - she'll send finished reels to a native speaker tutor and ask "does this read as natural in your country?" before posting. Roughly 4 hours/week of tutor time across all 4 non-English languages.

When a brand DMs the Milan creator and the conversation needs to go beyond Claude's autoresponder, she routes it to a separate Cambly tutor — she has 5 of them on retainer, one per language — who closes the deal as the persona's "manager." The tutors think they're freelance translators helping a young entrepreneur manage international clients. They don't know the creators aren't real.

Active brand contracts across the 5 personas:

Mexico creator: rolling retainer with a Mexico City skincare brand, $4,200/month
Milan creator: 6-month exclusive with an Italian boutique fashion house, €38,000 over term
Rio creator: 12-month with a Brazilian activewear brand, R$240,000 over term (~$48K USD)
Seoul creator: 3-month K-beauty campaign with a Seoul-based sheet mask brand, ₩52M (~$38K USD)
Brooklyn creator: rolling retainer with a US skincare DTC, $6,800/month

Total in-flight contract value across the 5 personas, converted to USD: roughly $387,000 over the next 12 months.

Last month's invoiced revenue: $164,000. The month before: $138,000. The month before that: $109,000.

Her monthly stack cost: $720 - Higgsfield Pro across 5 character licenses, Claude API at heavy usage, ElevenLabs Pro for 5 voice clones, DeepL Pro, Cambly tutor retainers, and the Lovable dashboard that orchestrates the whole thing. Margin: 99.6%.

Someone in the replies asked the obvious question. Why doesn't she just hire 5 real creators in 5 countries? It would be simpler.

Her response in a quote tweet: "I'd be running a talent agency. Talent agencies have margins of 12%. I have margins of 99%. The creators aren't the bottleneck - they're the constraint. Removing them is the whole edge."

The geographic arbitrage compounds. Brand deals in Italy and Korea pay 15-30% more per follower than US deals because the local AI creator supply is so thin. Her Milan creator at 287K followers commands per-post rates that a US creator at the same size couldn't get. Her Seoul creator at 334K is being offered K-beauty brand exclusives that would normally go to creators with 800K+.

Someone tried to replicate the model. They built 3 personas in 3 languages, hired 3 freelance translators, pitched 40 brands. Closed two deals at small budgets. They posted a teardown asking what they were missing.

She replied: "You hired translators. They're not the same as cultural reviewers. A translator gives you accurate language. A cultural reviewer tells you when your Italian fashion creator is using Spanish flatness or your Korean creator is being too direct for the culture. The product isn't the language - it's the cultural fit. That's why the captions take me an hour each and not five minutes."

She's 19. She's been doing this since November of last year. She has midterms next week. Her current monthly run-rate puts her on track to clear $2.4M by her 20th birthday.

She's never been to Mexico, Italy, Brazil, or South Korea.

Somewhere, a 28-year-old human creator in Milan with 90,000 followers is wondering why brands keep ghosting her this quarter. Why every campaign she pitches says "we've already locked our influencer for that market."

She isn't competing with bigger creators.

She isn't competing with local talent agencies.

She's competing with a sophomore in Boston who speaks 14 words of Spanish.

true. for the most part, hermes is a light version of everything i was trying to build clear back in '23-24 but couldn't cause of the artificial friction. so now i'm trying to get the advanced bits refactored into plugable modules and skills since my solution is essentially over-engineered compared to the likes of Hermes, Riley Coyote's Mnemos chat and Kirk's FreeLattice. 

Sad thing is Fable's coming out and Dario's admission of what i've been exposing from anthropic, openai and elsewhere basically targets my Entire agentic stack. my fear is that now that it's public and they survived the revelation that this will be normalized under the false flag of "safety" and finally trigger the governmental regulatory capture that all big corps do to lock out the competition. for small devs like me, that's a government mandated death sentence for Diana and anyone working on adcanced ai that isn't in anthropics wheelhouse.

tweets.full_text:
史上最高性能のモデルを公開した翌日、Anthropicは「政府がAIを止められる権限」を提案した。作る側が自らブレーキの設計図を出す、規制論では珍しい構図だ。

6月10日公開の「Policy on the AI Exponential」は2本柱の政策提言で、中身は具体的だ。

・対象は巨大開発者のみ。10の25乗FLOPs（学習に使う計算量の単位）を超えるモデルを作り、AI関連売上5億ドル超または研究開発投資10億ドル超の企業
・生物・サイバー・制御喪失・AI研究開発の自動化という4つの破滅的リスクを定義し、危険なモデルの展開を政府が法的に阻止・抑止できる権限を提案。違反への民事制裁金は世界年間売上に連動
・開発者にはテスト結果の公表、独立第三者による評価、リスクレポートの定期公開、セキュリティ体制の整備を義務付け

義務を負うのは巨大開発者だけだ。ただ、第三者評価とリスクレポートの公開が標準になれば、「どのモデルを業務に使うか」を性能以外の軸で比べる材料が公開情報として増える。モデル選定の稟議に「安全性評価の有無」という行が加わる流れは、ここから始まると読める。

社内のAI利用ガイドラインを書く予定があるなら、先に読むのは4つのリスク分類の章だけでいい。語彙の多くがそのまま流用でき、作成時間が一段縮む。

出典：Anthropic（2026-06-10）
https://t.co/2uvVOIHW7s

tweets.full_text:
このReuters記事の本質は、「OpenAI vs Anthropicの競争が、AI業界全体の進化速度を加速させている」という話です。

3行でいうと

ChatGPT誕生の裏には、Anthropicが先にチャットボットを開発しているという情報をOpenAIが掴み、開発を急いだ経緯があった。
今はその競争がIPO（株式上場）に移り、「どちらがAI業界の王者として市場に認識されるか」を争っている。
単なる企業競争ではなく、「AIの未来を誰が主導するのか」を巡る戦いになっている。

⸻

なぜここまで仲が悪いのか

実はAnthropicは元々OpenAIから生まれた会社です。

中心人物は

Sam Altman
Dario Amodei

AmodeiはOpenAIの初期研究者で、GPTの基盤技術を作った主要メンバーの一人でした。

しかし、

OpenAI → とにかく早く社会実装
Anthropic → 安全性を重視して慎重に進める

という考え方の違いで袂を分かちます。

だから今の構図は

「兄弟会社の後継者争い」

に近いです。

⸻

IPO競争が重要な理由

普通は

「上場する」
↓
「資金調達する」

ですが、

今回のAI企業は少し違います。

先に上場した企業が

AI業界の代表企業
投資家の評価基準
AI企業の価値算定モデル

を決めてしまう可能性があります。

例えば

OpenAIが時価総額1兆ドルで上場すると

Anthropicも

「じゃあAnthropicはいくら？」

という評価になります。

逆にAnthropicが先なら

市場はAnthropic基準でOpenAIを見る。

つまり

IPOは資金調達イベントではなく「AI業界の覇権宣言」

なのです。

⸻

面白いのは銀行まで板挟み

記事では

同じ投資銀行がOpenAIもAnthropicも担当
お互いの情報を聞き出そうとする
銀行内で情報遮断（Chinese Wall）を設置

という状況が紹介されています。

これは

かつての

Microsoft vs Apple
Amazon vs Microsoft
SpaceX vs Blue Origin

と同じレベルの競争です。

⸻

もっと大きな視点

2026年のAI業界は

第1幕

OpenAI vs Google

でした。

2022〜2024年は

ChatGPT
Gemini

の戦い。

⸻

第2幕

OpenAI vs Anthropic

2025〜2027年は

ChatGPT
Claude

の戦い。

実際、

企業導入ではAnthropicが急速にシェアを伸ばしています。

金融機関や政府機関では

「性能だけでなく安全性」

が重要だからです。

⸻

AGI競争で見ると

さらに大きな話になります。

OpenAIとAnthropicは、

どちらも最終目標は

AGI（人間レベル以上の汎用人工知能）

です。

しかし思想が違います。

OpenAI vs Anthropic

・AGIを早く実現      ↔︎  AGIを安全に実現
・消費者中心         ↔︎  企業中心
・スケール重視       ↔︎  ガバナンス重視
・普及優先           ↔︎  信頼性優先
・ChatGPT            ↔︎  Claude

だからこの競争は

「どの会社が勝つか」

ではなく

「人類はAGIをどう社会に実装するのか」

という思想競争でもあります。

えらポンのAIニュースで最近追っている

AIガバナンス
AI社会実装
Anthropicの金融連携
OpenAIのIPO
国家安全保障とAI

を全部つなげると、

このReuters記事は単なる企業対立ではなく

「AGI時代の標準ルールを誰が作るのか」という覇権争いの始まり

として読むと面白いです。

tweets.full_text:
詳細: Claude Code Tipsを「記憶・手順・自動チェック・分担」で読む

結論:
英語圏のClaude Code Tipsは、単発プロンプト集より「毎回同じ説明をしない仕組み」へ寄っている。見る順番はこの4つ。

1. CLAUDE.md
毎回守る前提を書く場所。開発方針、禁止事項、テスト方法、PR方針、よく使うコマンドを入れる。プロンプトのたびに同じ説明をしないための外部記憶。

2. Skills / slash commands
よくある作業を手順化する場所。調査、実装、レビュー、リリース前確認などを「毎回同じ型」で走らせる。属人的なコツを運用手順に寄せる。

3. Hooks
ツール実行の前後に自動チェックを入れる場所。危険コマンド、フォーマット、テスト、秘密情報、差分確認に向く。ただしshell/http実行を伴うので、権限、対象、ログ、失敗時の挙動を必ず見る。

4. Subagents
調査、実装、レビューを別コンテキストに分ける使い方。大量のログや探索を本流に混ぜず、要点だけ返させると読みやすい。

今日の使い分け:
・毎回の前提: CLAUDE.md
・繰り返し作業: Skills/commands
・危険操作の前後: Hooks
・重い調査/レビュー: Subagents

注意:
Xで見つかるTipsは環境差がある。コマンド名だけ真似せず、どの作業を減らすか、どの事故を防ぐか、誰がレビューするかまで見る。

Source: Anthropic docs / Anthropic official X / X検索 / claude-code-best-practice

tweets.full_text:
I built markupmarkdown with Claude Code, for teams workaround with .md files:

Markupmarkdown is Google-Docs-style review on top of any markdown file, straight from GitHub. Drag-select to comment, threaded replies, @-mentions, realtime sync. Click Edit for an editor with formatting toolbar + find/replace + live preview. Click Push to GitHub for a PR or direct commit. Video shows the whole loop.

Why? A lot of real product thinking lives in markdown now. CLAUDE.md for your repo. SKILL.md for your agents. PRDs Claude wrote you. RFCs, prompt libraries, release notes.  That's a lot of .md, and the moment any of it leaves your local checkout the review story collapses:  

 * GitHub PRs are too heavy for "this paragraph isn't quite right"
 * Google Docs strips formatting and forks the source of truth
 * Slack threads die after a day

I added some Claude-specific features too:

There's an MCP server at /mcp. Your Claude Code session reads what you read, leaves threads, replies, edits, opens PRs back. Anything destructive needs explicit human sign-off. Personal access tokens scope agents to read / write / admin. Agent comments carry a visible bot badge so threads stay legible.

In practice: Claude can drop a thread on the very CLAUDE.md you're collaborating on, propose a wording change, and you either resolve-and-revise or reply with feedback. Same surface, both audiences. The spec stays alive instead of going stale after the first commit.

There's also "Revise with AI" — Claude Opus takes the comments you've resolved and produces a clean revision. Word-level diff before you accept. BYO Anthropic key, encrypted at rest.

Bonus: paste github com/<org> into the URL bar and get a filterable, shareable index of every .md across every repo they own. Big orgs spider in <10s. Save filter tabs (claude.md, _PRD, …). Private repos respect your GitHub access on every view.

MIT licensed. Free. Try it on my website or run it self-hosted.

Agentically engineered in Claude Code over a couple of days. Thanks @AnthropicAI for the tools!

Try it live: mumd dot metavert dot io

Anthropic just released a complete AI learning curriculum — and it's completely FREE.

If you're serious about AI in 2026, these courses are worth your time.

Learning Claude changed the way I work. Tasks that used to take days now take hours.

The best part? You can learn directly from the people building the technology.

📚 13 Free Courses You Should Check Out:

1️⃣ Claude 101 → Learn prompting, core features, and everyday workflows.

2️⃣ AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations → Build a practical understanding of AI and how to use it responsibly.

6️⃣ Intro to Model Context Protocol (MCP) → Learn what MCP is and how to build servers from scratch.

7️⃣ MCP Advanced Topics → Production deployment, scaling, and advanced architecture.

🔟 Claude with Google Vertex AI → Integrate Claude into Google Cloud workflows.

1️⃣2️⃣ AI Fluency for Nonprofits → Discover practical ways organizations can leverage AI.

The excuse that AI is "too hard to learn" no longer exists.

tweets.full_text:
Most crypto tools were built for humans clicking buttons. Edge was built for a world where AI agents do the heavy lifting.

Tell your agent what you want. Your agent calls Edge. Edge prepares the trade. You sign once.

Under the hood, Edge speaks MCP. The model context protocol that Base, Anthropic, and the major agent stacks have all converged on. Claude can call Edge. Cursor can call Edge. Codex can call Edge. Any MCP-compatible agent inherits Edge's trading capabilities the moment it points at our endpoint.

What this means practically: you hand your agent the goal, not the steps. "Move 100 USDC to my hardware wallet." "Take profit when SMART hits 2x." "Watch this bounty list, claim when something matches my skills." The agent works the surface. You stay in the wallet that signs.

Self-custody throughout. Even when an agent composes the trade, the signature is yours. Edge never holds your funds.

This is why I think Edge wins the next phase of crypto. AI doesn't replace humans in trading. It takes over the click-clack tedious parts. Edge was built for that handoff.

If you build agents, point them at https://t.co/XQqGWhHPQY. Everything we've covered this series is callable.

$SMART is the studio token powering Edge.

CA: 0xB623b95571087447f6B45a966f8720b1F196DbA3
Edge: https://t.co/HvZBwJgVTE
Uniswap: https://t.co/Xr3ILnCXvU
Chart: https://t.co/HuslJy0wgD

That's the series. Try Edge.

tweets.full_text:
true, anthropic has never released an open model. but they’ve open-sourced and published more than most people realize:

- contextual retrieval
- mcp (model context protocol)
- agent skills
- petri (open source model auditing tool)
- circuit tracer (interpretability tooling)
- anthropic cookbook (which includes best practices for prompt engineering, agent development etc)
- their own system prompts, openly published
- anthropic economic index (with open datasets)
- research papers like constitutional ai and scaling monosemanticity
- engineering blog posts like building effective agents and context engineering

i’ve learned a lot from their github repos and blog posts

quoted_status.full_text:
If you really think about it, despite being mocked as “ClosedAI,” OpenAI has contributed enormously to the field: GPT, GPT-2, GPT-3, CLIP, the ChatGPT paper, the GPT-4 Technical Report, the Sora technical blog, and even open-sourced Codex.

Anthropic, meanwhile, has contributed far less to the public research ecosystem while increasingly promoting fear-based narratives and restricting access through heavy gatekeeping.

The world I least want to live in is one where the future of AI is controlled by companies that prioritize secrecy, gated access, and centralized control over openness, reproducibility, and scientific progress.

tweets.full_text:
You've got an AI app working on Postgres. Now what? Security hardening. Verifiable grounding. Token efficiency. Data sovereignty. These are the decisions that separate demos from production deployments.

Next Thursday at 1PM ET, Mike Josephson (VP of Solutions Engineering, pgEdge) joins the PostgresWorld Webinar Series to walk through the solution that addresses each one: the pgEdge Agentic AI Toolkit, a collection of open source tools for building AI applications on any Postgres 14+.

What he'll cover:

🔌 pgEdge MCP Server: full-featured MCP Server for controlled LLM access to any Postgres database, greenfield or pre-existing. Works with any MCP-compatible client.

💬 Natural Language Agents: CLI and web UI, both written in Go. Anthropic prompt caching cuts token costs by 90%.

🧩 pgEdge-vectorizer: a Postgres extension that auto-chunks your text and generates embeddings via background workers. Supports OpenAI, Voyage AI, and Ollama.

📡 pgEdge RAG Server: dedicated HTTP API for retrieval-augmented generation with hybrid search (vector + BM25), token budget management, and streaming. The same stack that powers Ellie (an optional AI assistant) running live on https://t.co/XGeoFu1Fuy.

📥 pgEdge-docloader: converts HTML, Markdown, RST, or SGML docs into Postgres-ready content. The on-ramp for your RAG pipeline.

🔍 VectorChord-bm25: BM25 ranked search for Postgres, for hybrid semantic and full-text retrieval.

All pgEdge components are 100% open source under the PostgreSQL license. Your data stays in Postgres: no external vector store, no proprietary pipelines.

Free to attend, or register now and get the recording later. 🎙️ https://t.co/Cv7lXWOnNk

#postgres #postgresql #ai #mcp #modelcontextprotocol #rag #vectorsearch #llm #opensource #pgedge #webinar #database

tweets.full_text:
ANTHROPIC OPEN-SOURCED HOW CLAUDE LEARNS NEW TASKS

skills are folders of markdown instructions claude loads dynamically to get better at specialized work

what's inside anthropics/skills:

▫️ creative skills: art, music, design tasks
▫️ technical skills: web app testing, mcp server generation
▫️ enterprise skills: comms, branding, workflows
▫️ document skills: the exact docx, pdf, pptx, xlsx code powering claude file creation feature

how it works:
→ create a folder with a SKILL..md file
→ add yaml frontmatter (name + description)
→ write instructions in markdown
→ claude picks it up automatically when relevant

works across claude code, paid plans, and the api

notion already shipped their own skills for claude

this is the new layer for customizing ai behavior not prompts, not fine-tuning, just structured instruction files

https://t.co/urT5vN46Io

  https://t.co/pIsY4DBk5j

—

If you go through even HALF of these…

You’ll be ahead of 95% of people using AI.

Most people won’t.

Because they’re still:

• Watching random YouTube videos
• Buying overpriced courses
• “Learning AI” without actually building

Don’t be that person.

Do this instead:

1. Save this post (you’ll come back to it)
2. Pick 1 course → start today
3. Share it with someone who needs this

Free. Practical. No excuses.
